itemListElement.name: What’s the difference between a cosmetic surgeon and a plastic surgeon?

@graph.abstract: This article explains the differences between plastic surgeons and cosmetic surgeons, focusing on the training, residency programs, board certifications, and safety standards associated with each path. It outlines how board-eligible and board-certified plastic surgeons complete extensive accredited residency training in reconstructive and cosmetic surgery through programs recognized by the American Board of Medical Specialties (ABMS) and the American Board of Plastic Surgery (ABPS). The article also discusses how cosmetic surgeons may come from unrelated medical specialties with varying levels of surgical training. Additionally, it highlights the specialized training pathways of oculoplastic surgeons and ENT-trained facial plastic surgeons. Overall, the article helps patients understand how surgical training, accreditation, and board certification can impact safety, expertise, and surgical outcomes when choosing a surgeon.
keywords: how to choose a plastic surgeon

@graph.articleBody: Plastic Surgeon vs. Cosmetic Surgeon Did you know a cosmetic surgeon isn’t the same as a plastic surgeon? In fact, the training behind each title can be very different. In some cases, those differences matter when you’re choosing who to trust with your care. A board-eligible or board-certified plastic surgeon has followed a clearly defined path: four years of medical school, 6–8 years of accredited residency training in both reconstructive and cosmetic surgery, and thousands of hours in the operating room under supervision. This is all part of a plastic surgery residency program that is accredited and designed to prepare surgeons for both plastic and reconstructive surgeries as well as aesthetic procedures. Board-eligible plastic surgeons are fully trained and practicing while preparing to take their exams with the American Board of Plastic Surgery (ABPS), a 1-2 year process. Board-certified plastic surgeons have already passed those exams, earning an additional credential that confirms they are certified in plastic surgery and recognized as certified plastic surgeons with proven expertise, surgical skill, and commitment to lifelong learning. A cosmetic surgeon, on the other hand, may come from a different medical specialty altogether. Some are trained in dermatology, family practice, gynecology, or another field, and then later choose to perform aesthetic surgery or cosmetic surgical procedures such as breast augmentation. While cosmetic surgeons may be experienced in the treatments they offer, their training program in surgery can vary in length, scope, and setting, and most do not spend any time in a plastic surgery residency program. In fact, some cosmetic surgeons have no formal, specialized training in the procedures they are offering. This is possible because the American Board of Cosmetic Surgery is not an accredited governing board and is not held to the same standards as the American Board of Medical Specialties (ABMS). The most rigorous boards are those that fall under the ABMS. These boards act as governing bodies to ensure physicians practice medicine with the highest ethical and professional standards. When we talk about board-eligible and board-certified plastic surgeons, we’re specifically referring to the American Board of Plastic Surgery (ABPS), which is a member of the ABMS and recognized as the gold standard. By contrast, other boards that are not part of the ABMS can be misleading. They may sound official, but they lack the strict requirements, residency programs, and oversight that patients assume are in place. That’s why the type of board truly matters. A Note About Oculoplastic Surgeons and ENT-Facial Plastic Surgeons It’s also important to understand that not all surgeons offering aesthetic procedures fall into the category of cosmetic surgeons. Oculoplastic surgeons begin their training in ophthalmology and then complete additional fellowship training that focuses on the eyes, eyelids, orbit, and surrounding facial structures. This makes them uniquely skilled in delicate procedures around the eyes where precision is critical. ENT-trained facial plastic surgeons (otolaryngologists) complete residency training in ear, nose, and throat surgery before pursuing specialized fellowship training in facial plastic and reconstructive surgery. Their expertise is centered on procedures such as rhinoplasty, facelift, and reconstructive surgery of the head and neck. Both of these paths involve rigorous, accredited specialization training programs that are very different from cosmetic surgery alone. Unlike cosmetic surgeons, whose training may not include formal surgical residencies, oculoplastic and ENT-trained facial plastic surgeons follow structured, accredited pathways that emphasize both function and aesthetics, ensuring a strong foundation in patient care. Why These Differences Matter for You as a Patient As a patient, knowing these distinctions isn’t just about terminology. In many cases, it directly impacts your safety, results, and overall experience. Surgeons with accredited, residency-based training (such as board-eligible and board-certified plastic surgeons, oculoplastic surgeons, and ENT-trained facial plastic surgeons) are prepared to manage not only the procedure itself but also any complications that might arise. This depth of training means they have: Extensive clinical and surgical experience across a wide range of surgical procedures. Hospital privileges, which allow them to care for you in accredited medical centers if needed. A foundation in both reconstructive and aesthetic surgery, ensuring results that look natural and preserve or restore proper function. Rigorous safety standards require them to operate in accredited facilities with qualified anesthesia providers. In contrast, a cosmetic surgeon without formal residency or specialization training may not have the same background in managing complications or the same level of oversight. This doesn’t mean every cosmetic surgeon delivers poor results, but it does mean the variability in training can create a higher risk for patients. When you understand the difference in pathways, you’re better equipped to evaluate credentials, ask the right questions, and choose a surgeon who prioritizes your safety as much as your outcome. How to Choose the Right Plastic Surgeon for You Your comfort, trust, and safety are the most important when choosing the right surgeon for you. Here are some tips to help you feel confident in your choice: Check credentials: Look up whether your surgeon is a cosmetic surgeon or board-certified/board-eligible with the American Board of Plastic Surgery. Membership in the American Society of Plastic Surgeons or the American Society of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery is another strong sign of training and accountability. Ask about training: Find out where they completed their residency and ensure that it is an accredited training program by the American Board of Medical Specialties. Review before-and-after photos: Make sure you like their aesthetic style and see results that align with your goals. Meet Virtually or In-Person: The consultation is your chance to gauge the surgeon’s communication style, how they answer questions, and how comfortable you feel in their care. Ask about safety: Confirm the procedure will be performed in an accredited surgical facility with appropriate anesthesia providers. At the end of the day, choosing a surgeon is about more than titles. It’s about trust, training, and how comfortable you feel in their care. Understanding the difference between board-eligible, board-certified, and cosmetic surgeons gives you the clarity to ask the right questions and make the choice that feels right for you. Whether you’re just starting your research or ready to schedule a consultation, knowing your surgeon’s background ensures you can move forward with confidence, peace of mind, and excitement about your results.

itemListElement.name: Preservé Breast Augmentation vs. Traditional Breast Augmentation

@graph.abstract: This article compares Preservé breast augmentation and traditional breast augmentation, explaining the benefits, recovery differences, implant options, and ideal candidates for each technique. Preservé is a newer, less invasive breast augmentation approach that uses Motiva SmoothSilk Ergonomix implants placed above the muscle to preserve natural breast tissue, reduce muscle disruption, shorten recovery time, and create subtle, natural-looking results often described as “yoga boobs” or “ballerina boobs.” Traditional breast augmentation remains a highly effective and customizable option, offering greater flexibility in implant size, profile, placement, and implant brands, making it ideal for patients seeking larger enhancements, more dramatic results, or combination procedures like a breast lift. The article emphasizes that both approaches are safe and effective, and the best choice depends on a patient’s anatomy, goals, lifestyle, and desired recovery experience.

mainEntity.name: What is Preservé breast augmentation surgery?#faq-q1

acceptedAnswer.text: Preservé breast augmentation is a less invasive technique that uses a special device designed to place Motiva SmoothSilk Ergonomix® implants with minimal disruption to the natural breast tissue and chest muscles. Preservé is known for its natural-looking results (think “yoga boobs” or “ballerina boobs”), faster recovery, and slightly smaller scars. It’s ideal for those seeking their first breast augmentation with an active lifestyle interested in going up just 1-2 cup sizes. Though it is possible to have a breast lift with Preservé, the technique’s benefits are most effective for those who do not need or want a lift to achieve their goals. #faq-a1

mainEntity.name: How is Preservé surgery performed?#faq-q2

acceptedAnswer.text: Preservé breast augmentation is performed by board-certified plastic surgeons, often “awake”, using mild sedation instead of general anesthesia. That means you’re comfortable, safe, and often back to your usual routine faster. Through a small incision (~2.5cm) in the breast crease, the implant is placed above the muscle (subglandular placement) rather than under the muscle itself, leaving the chest muscle intact. Placing the implant over the muscle may help reduce the animation or movement of the implant when the chest muscles are activated.  This approach protects your chest muscles, the functionality of the natural breast tissue, and may reduce the risk of changes in sensation. Additionally, Motiva implants may reduce inflammation and complications, such as capsular contracture, and the Preservé technique can improve implant stability.#faq-a2

mainEntity.name: What are the benefits of Preservé?#faq-q3

acceptedAnswer.text: One of the biggest benefits of Preservé is the shorter recovery time. Many patients find they are able to have their procedure in the morning and still go out for dinner that evening. Preservé patients are typically cleared to go back to their normal exercise routines in as little as 1-2 weeks post-op, making it ideal for those with an active, busy lifestyle.Many of these patients also see their results develop sooner than traditional breast augmentation.#faq-a3

mainEntity.name: Who is a good candidate for Preservé?#faq-q4

acceptedAnswer.text: Preservé isn’t the right choice for everyone, but it can be a wonderful option if you’re looking for a natural look and feel, want a quicker, easier recovery, or don’t necessarily want your implants placed under the muscle. To take full advantage of the shorter recovery and less invasive approach, though it is possible to include a breast lift, Preservé is typically better suited for those that do not require a breast lift to achieve their goals. The Preservé method uses Motiva SmoothSilk Ergonomix® silicone implants with sizes ranging from 150cc-315cc, making the ideal Preservé patients those looking for a subtle increase in volume (1-2 bra cup size increase). During your consultation, your surgeon will take the time to review your goals, look at your anatomy, and walk you through your options. This ensures you get a personalized plan that helps you decide whether Preservé or a traditional approach will give you the results you’re hoping for.#faq-a4

mainEntity.name: What is traditional breast augmentation?#faq-q5

acceptedAnswer.text: For decades, traditional breast augmentation has been performed, and it is a safe, effective option that’s helped millions of women feel more confident.  Traditional breast augmentation is the classic method of placing breast implants, often under the chest muscle (submuscular), though implants can also be placed under the fascia (subfascial placement) or above the muscle (subglandular placement). The traditional method allows for more flexibility in implant placement, implant type (silicone or saline implants), implant size, implant profile, and implant brand. These options allow for more customization based on your unique anatomy to achieve your desired look. Traditional breast augmentation is a great choice for those seeking a more augmented look, larger implants, saline implants, desire a specific brand, want more customization options with their implants, or need a breast lift to achieve their goals. But because traditional breast augmentation is typically performed under general anesthesia and often requires more manipulation of the muscle, the recovery can sometimes be a little longer and more uncomfortable. To support patients during this time, Amelia specializes in “Rapid Recovery,” a recovery plan that helps increase mobility to make your recovery faster and more comfortable. Many patients find they are back to work in 3-5 days after breast augmentation and are cleared to resume heavy-lifting/exercise 6 weeks post-op. Depending on the placement, final results after traditional breast augmentation can take up to a year to “drop and fluff” to the optimal results. #faq-a5

mainEntity.name: Is Preservé breast augmentation recovery different from traditional breast augmentation recovery?#faq-q6

acceptedAnswer.text: Preservé breast augmentation is known for a quicker, less intense recovery, and is often preferred by those with an active lifestyle. Since the chest muscle isn't disrupted, many patients report minimal discomfort and return to light activities within 1–2 days. Subglandular (over the muscle) placement also often reduces or eliminates the “drop and fluff” process, allowing for results to develop more quickly. Bruising and swelling may occur but typically fade over the first two weeks. Many patients resume full exercise in 2 weeks and water activities by 6 weeks, with results continuing to refine over the next few months. Traditional breast augmentation generally requires more downtime. Because the muscle is often involved, patients typically take 3-5 days off work. Activities like lifting, swimming, or high-intensity movement are usually paused for about 6 weeks, and results may take up to 12 months to develop.Both procedures require proper scar care and follow-up, but Preservé breast augmentation tends to offer a faster return to daily life with less discomfort along the way.#faq-a6

mainEntity.name: Why should I choose traditional breast augmentation?#faq-q7

acceptedAnswer.text: Traditional augmentation is trusted, flexible, and familiar. It offers a wide range of options that allow you to fully customize your outcome. You might prefer traditional augmentation if you’re looking for: More flexibility in implant type and size: Including higher-volume implants or specific brands. Greater control over implant placement and profile: More options for how your implants look and feel. Muscle coverage for added support: Especially helpful if you have less existing breast tissue. A long-standing, well-documented technique: With predictable results and a large body of clinical support. Suitability for more complex cases, such as revision surgeries or combining with a breast lift.#faq-a7

mainEntity.name: How do I know whether Preservé or traditional is right for me?#faq-q8

acceptedAnswer.text: There’s no universal “best” procedure. The right one depends on your body, your goals, and how you want to feel. You might be a good candidate for Preservé breast augmentation if: You’re interested in a gentle, natural result with smaller implants. This is your first augmentation. You want to reduce the risk of changes in sensation. You like the idea of avoiding general anesthesia/being awake for your procedure. You may be better suited for traditional breast augmentation if: You’re looking for a larger size increase or a more augmented look. You’re combining with a breast lift or doing a revision. You want more control over your implant profile, size, brand, or type. You’re comfortable with general anesthesia and a longer recovery.#faq-a8

mainEntity.name: How do I choose between traditional and Preservé breast augmentation?#faq-q9

acceptedAnswer.text: Both traditional and Preservé methods are safe and effective methods of breast augmentation. The right method for you is the one that is truly best for your goals, anatomy, and lifestyle. But you don’t have to figure it out alone. You and your surgeon will thoroughly discuss your goals and anatomy without pressure or judgment during your consultation. You’ll have the opportunity to ask questions based on your unique anatomy, discuss any concerns, and create an individualized plan. Whether your vision is subtle and sculpted or full and transformative, your surgeon is there to help you understand your options and support you in choosing what aligns with your lifestyle and goals.#faq-a9

@graph.articleBody: What does board-certified vs. board-eligible plastic surgeon mean? When you start researching plastic surgery, you’ll likely come across terms such as board-certified or board-eligible. But what do these titles actually mean, and why do they matter for patients considering breast augmentation, cosmetic surgery, or other procedures? First, it’s worth defining what board-certified or board eligible means in medicine. There are many different kinds of boards, and not all of them carry the same weight. Some are created by nonprofit organizations with rigorous standards, while others are established by smaller governing bodies with very loose requirements. Depending on the type of board a surgeon belongs to, the level of training, oversight, and patient care requirements can look very different. Understanding the Role of Governing Boards The most rigorous boards are those that fall under the American Board of Medical Specialties (ABMS). These boards act as governing bodies to ensure physicians practice medicine with the highest ethical and professional standards. The ABMS works much like a governing board or board of directors in a nonprofit organization. Its job is to set rules, oversee quality, and maintain public trust. By contrast, other boards that are not part of the ABMS can be misleading. They may sound official, but they lack the strict requirements, residency programs, and oversight that patients assume are in place. That’s why the type of board truly matters. When we talk about board-eligible and board-certified plastic surgeons, we’re specifically referring to the American Board of Plastic Surgery (ABPS), which is a member of the ABMS and recognized as the gold standard. Board-Eligible vs. Board-Certified at a Glance So, what’s the difference between a board-eligible plastic surgeon and a board-certified plastic surgeon, and what does it mean for you as a patient? The good news is that both board-eligible and board-certified plastic surgeons have already completed years of training and clinical experience in accredited medical centers and residency programs. Both groups represent doctors who have dedicated a decade or more of full-time education and practice to this specialty. Board-Eligible Plastic Surgeon Completed medical school and an accredited plastic surgery training program Fully trained and qualified to practice plastic surgery Has not yet taken, or is awaiting results from, the American Board of Plastic Surgery (ABPS) exams. (a 1-2 year process) Often in the earlier years of independent practice Meets the same high standards of training as board-certified surgeons Board-Certified Plastic Surgeon Completed medical school and plastic surgery residency training Fully trained and qualified to practice plastic surgery Has passed the rigorous ABPS written and oral exams Typically, more established in practice Holds an additional credential proving mastery, ethics, and ongoing education No matter the title, both board-certified and board-eligible surgeons have walked the same path: Medical School: Four years of advanced medical education. Residency Training: 6–8 years of general or plastic surgery training. Hands-On Experience: Thousands of hours in the operating room, performing both reconstructive and cosmetic procedures under supervision. Accredited Programs: Training programs overseen by the American Council for Graduate Medical Education (ACGME), with strict faculty oversight, ensuring strict standards for patient care and surgical safety. By the time they’re ready to practice on their own, these surgeons have already spent nearly a decade treating patients in every type of setting, from life-saving trauma and burn repair to cosmetic breast surgery and facial rejuvenation. Why This Matters for You For patients, this distinction matters because it provides peace of mind. Whether your surgeon is board-eligible or board-certified, you can trust they have gone through extensive training, rigorous residency programs, and years of clinical practice and medical experience. Board-eligible surgeons are fully trained, capable, and often earlier in their career paths while awaiting certification. Board-certified surgeons have the added credential of passing their exams and ongoing education, which serves as an extra layer of accountability. In either case, patients benefit from the surgeon’s years of training, their focus on safety, and their dedication to excellent patient care. At Amelia Aesthetics, we believe that board certification is only part of the story. Along with the high standards set by the American Board of Plastic Surgery, Amelia surgeons are held to an additional set of values, ones that focus on empathy, collaboration, and your overall experience. Just as advisory board members in nonprofit organizations help guide missions and protect integrity, Amelia surgeons are selected not only for their technical expertise but also for their warmth, kindness, and commitment to patient care. From breast augmentation to multi-stage skin removal procedures, you’ll find that Amelia’s surgeons take time to listen, understand your goals, and design a safe, personalized treatment plan. From your first consultation to your recovery, Amelia’s approach is collaborative and patient-centered. We know that choosing plastic surgery is a deeply personal decision. That’s why we foster an environment where patients feel empowered, informed, and supported at every step. Whether your surgeon is board-eligible or board-certified, the journey to becoming a plastic surgeon involves years of training, full-time residency programs, clinical experience in medical centers, and oversight from the most respected governing bodies in health care. But at Amelia, you’ll find more than just credentials. You’ll find partners who care deeply about your goals and well-being. Because at the end of the day, exceptional surgical outcomes are important, but so is the way you feel throughout your journey. And at Amelia, you don’t have to choose between the two. You get both.

keywords: how to prepare for plastic surgery
preparing your body for plastic surgery

@graph.articleBody: Ways to Optimize Your Plastic Surgery Results Congratulations! You've made the empowering decision to pour into yourself and undergo plastic surgery. As you anticipate and plan for your upcoming cosmetic procedure, you're likely focused on the exciting results ahead. Did you know that getting your body and mind ready can help you recover better? It can also improve end results. This proactive approach, sometimes called prehabilitation, involves taking simple yet impactful steps to optimize your physical and mental state. Let's explore four key ways to set yourself up for the best possible outcome. 1. Fuel Your Body With Smart Nutrition & Hydration Surgery and the recovery process are a lot like running a marathon. Your body is working hard around the clock to heal, and needs fuel to do so. Fuel comes in the way of good nutrition, adequate calories, and hydration. A strong foundation means nourishing your body before surgery, giving you a head start towards better results. Here are some nutrition tips that can make a difference in how well your body recovers: Focus on a balanced diet rich in protein, fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. Don't forget to hydrate consistently with plenty of water. Avoid processed foods and added sugars, including those in beverages. Limit unhealthy fats and excessive sodium before and after surgery. Think of this as a season of more—more nourishment (specifically, protein and fiber), more rest, and more hydration. This small window of time sets the course for long-term health and healing for your best result. 2. Gently Build a Solid Physical Foundation Gentle exercise and rest also play a big role in your healing and results. As you now know, your body is active during surgery and recovery. Building muscle and improving heart health before surgery sets your body up for a smoother recovery and better results. The good news is your routine doesn't need to include intense workouts. Consistent, low-impact activities like walking, swimming, or cycling support your cardiovascular health, too. Incorporating gentle core exercises into your routine prior to surgery can build strength and aid stability, especially important after abdominal procedures like a tummy tuck. Light strength training and flexibility exercises can also contribute to a more comfortable recovery and improve results. Not sure how to start an exercise routine? Sometimes that's the hardest part. Group classes and one-on-one sessions with a personal trainer are a great first step, providing structure, community, and support as you learn what works best for you and your body. 3. Nurture Your Mental & Emotional Well-being In many cases, the decision to have plastic surgery is stretched and considered over a long period. When the time finally comes, and you begin preparing for cosmetic surgery, it can bring a mix of emotions—excitement, anxiety, guilt, and uncertainty (all completely normal!). As you get ready for your big day, it’s important to go in mentally prepared with realistic expectations. Waiting for Results You’ll likely find that healing isn’t linear and no two recoveries are the same. If your results aren’t developing exactly like that social media influencer, that’s because everyone’s journey, anatomy, and procedure are different! Many plastic surgery procedure results take a year to develop, meaning what you see in the mirror on day 2 (and even month 2) isn’t your true results. Implants take time to drop and fluff and settle into their final position. Swelling from liposuction and tummy tucks fluctuates in the first year post-op. In fact, some find it helpful to avoid the scale for the first few months after surgery as swelling can impact the number on the scale. Added stress and anxiety, also known as post-op blues, can creep in as we ebb and flow through the healing process. You might consider writing a letter to yourself before surgery, sharing why you chose to start this journey in the first place. It can also be helpful to take a before photo and weekly progress photos to see all the changes your body is making. Acknowledging and planning for the mental highs and lows in advance can help you be kind to your body as it heals. It can also improve your mental health and may give you a better perspective on all the changes your body has made. Planning Ahead The initial downtime of your recovery is the start of your results. The more you rest, focus on healing, and follow your surgeon’s recovery plan, the better your results will likely be. Be intentional about giving yourself the space and time to focus on rest and healing. Make a plan ahead of time and find someone to help with daily tasks like meals, car pools, and other duties to eliminate added stress. Get Support Lean on your support network and connect with others who have gone through or are going through the process in supportive, plastic surgery communities like Bustmob. Additionally, don’t hesitate to openly discuss any concerns with your surgical team. Many of them have been in your shoes, surgery patients preparing for surgery, and are there to help you feel comfortable and informed. If you feel something is off, have a question, or need help, your clinical team is your go-to resource. Remember, there are no weird or wrong questions! 4. Optimize Your Lifestyle Habits for Healing Taking care of your body pre- and post-operatively is important. Certain habits can significantly affect your body's ability to heal. If you smoke or vape, making an effort to quit or reduce use can help exponentially in the healing process. Nicotine hinders blood flow and increases the risk of postoperative complications. Limiting alcohol intake and establishing a consistent sleep schedule can also support optimal recovery for better results. By embracing these proactive steps before your plastic surgery, you're not only preparing your body for the procedure itself but also laying the groundwork for a more comfortable and successful recovery. Your primary care physician is an excellent resource for navigating the cutback and quitting journey. They can provide personalized guidance and support based on your health history and goals. Always follow your surgeon and clinical team’s protocol. The information in this article is a general guideline and is not intended to serve as medical advice. If you’re struggling with mental or physical health, contact your healthcare provider. During your consultation, your surgeon will walk you through your unique surgical plan, recovery protocol, and can give you individualized instructions on how to prepare your body and mind ahead of time. Taking these actionable steps empowers you to be an active participant in your journey towards your smoothest recovery and optimal results.

@graph.abstract: This page explores the difference between medspa treatments and plastic surgery, helping patients understand when nonsurgical options may no longer provide meaningful results. Dr. Aspinder Singh explains common aesthetic concerns such as under-eye hollows, neck laxity, jowling, skin laxity, and facial aging, while outlining which treatments work best for each issue. The content emphasizes a natural, patient-centered approach, comparing treatments like PRF, fat grafting, lip filler, Botox, lasers, facelifts, brow lifts, neck lifts, and liposuction. It also highlights how modern facial plastic surgery techniques create longer-lasting, more predictable, and natural-looking outcomes when compared to injectables or skincare alone. Throughout the article, readers are encouraged to seek honest guidance from experienced providers who can recommend both surgical and nonsurgical solutions based on anatomy, goals, and long-term results.
keywords: when to get a facelift instead of filler

mainEntity.name: When should I move from medspa treatments to surgery?#faq-q1

acceptedAnswer.text: Consider surgery when you’re no longer seeing meaningful results or when your anatomy requires something stronger than skincare, injectables, or devices. If you’re experiencing diminishing returns from injectables—meaning each treatment helps less than the one before—it may be a sign that you’ve reached the limit of what nonsurgical options can do. Some signs of aging come from deeper structural changes that fillers, toxins, or skin treatments simply cannot correct, such as sagging tissues or volume loss.  If you’re looking for longer-lasting, more predictable results, surgical procedures often provide outcomes that nonsurgical treatments cannot replicate.#faq-a1

mainEntity.name: What causes under-eye hollows, darkness, or bags—and what treatment works best?#faq-q2

acceptedAnswer.text: Under-eye concerns come from thin skin, fat changes, and muscle anatomy. These causes can typically be addressed with PRF, fat grafting, skin treatments, or surgery. Traditional fillers can often look unnatural in the under-eye area because the skin is extremely thin, and any material placed beneath it may appear puffy, visible, or discolored. More natural-looking options include PRF injections, which release growth factors slowly, and fat grafting, which restores soft volume using your own tissue. Skin-improving treatments like microneedling and chemical peels can help thicken the skin, brighten darkness, and smooth fine lines. True under-eye bags, caused by fat pads pushing forward, require lower blepharoplasty surgery to reposition or remove the fat for the most effective result.#faq-a2

mainEntity.name: What does a surgical brow lift do that Botox cannot?#faq-q3

acceptedAnswer.text: A brow lift repositions tissue long-term, while wrinkle relaxers, like Botox, only relax muscles temporarily. Botox works chemically by weakening specific muscle fibers to create a soft, subtle lift, but it cannot physically reposition tissue. A surgical brow lift, on the other hand, elevates the brow structurally, restoring youthful positioning that Botox alone cannot achieve. Patients who no longer get the same lifting or brightening effect from Botox often benefit more predictably from surgery. Unlike neurotoxin/wrinkle relaxer results—which typically last around three to four months—a brow lift offers improvements that last for many years.#faq-a3

mainEntity.name: When is it time for a facelift?#faq-q4

acceptedAnswer.text: A facelift is best when deeper structures—like cheeks, jowls, and neck—start to sag beyond what injectables can correct. Signs such as jowling, deep folds around the mouth, or loss of cheek volume signal that the underlying facial structures have shifted and require a surgical approach. Patients in their 40s,50s, and 60s who experience noticeable sagging and flattening of their features may be excellent candidates for a facelift.#faq-a4

mainEntity.name: What is facelift recovery like today?#faq-q5

acceptedAnswer.text: Modern deep-plane facelifts heal faster and look more natural than older techniques. Today’s surgical approach lifts the skin and muscle together as a single unit, which maintains blood supply, reduces tension, and leads to more stable healing. Because of these improved techniques, patients experience significantly less bruising and swelling than facelifts from decades past. Most patients feel comfortable returning to social activities within one to three weeks, with continued refinement over time.#faq-a5

mainEntity.name: What can be done for neck laxity or fullness?#faq-q6

acceptedAnswer.text: Early aging responds to Botox and skincare, but true contouring requires a neck lift. In the early stages, nonsurgical treatments such as lasers, microneedling, retinol, and Botox to soften neck bands can improve skin texture and mild laxity. For more advanced concerns, a deep neck lift can precisely address deeper fat deposits, enlarged glands, prominent muscles, or platysmal banding. Surgical neck contouring offers the most predictable and transformative long-term improvement, especially when multiple deeper structures contribute to the fullness.#faq-a6

mainEntity.name: Which is better for under-chin fat: Kybella, liposuction, or a neck lift?#faq-q7

acceptedAnswer.text: For many patients concerned by a double chin/submental fullness, Kybella is generally not recommended. Liposuction addresses fullness and is typically best for those without significant excess skin. A neck lift addresses fullness, excess skin, and underlying muscle separation for complete contouring. Kybella tends to produce unpredictable results because it dissolves fat non-selectively, and it also carries a risk of nerve irritation or prolonged swelling. Double chin liposuction can be a great option for patients who have isolated fat under the chin and naturally good skin elasticity. A neck lift provides the most comprehensive and sculpted result by addressing not only fat but also glands, muscle, and skin laxity for a more defined jawline.#faq-a7

mainEntity.name: What makes a good medspa?#faq-q8

acceptedAnswer.text: A great medspa combines skilled providers with a surgeon who can offer guidance when nonsurgical options are no longer enough. A well-run medspa should have a dedicated medical director who oversees safety, protocols, and treatment planning. Its providers should have a deep understanding of facial anatomy, how to perform treatments, and when surgery is needed to achieve your goals. A quality medspa should also offer a full range of treatment options, including both nonsurgical solutions and easy access to surgical consultations when appropriate. Most importantly, patients should receive honest, pressure-free guidance rather than being upsold products or treatments that aren’t the best fit for their goals.#faq-a8

mainEntity.name: Is neurotoxin (Botox) resistance real?#faq-q9

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, patients can experience reduced responsiveness, but switching brands often helps.Long-term use of neurotoxins can gradually decrease their effectiveness, causing results to fade sooner or lifting effects to weaken. Different wrinkle relaxer brands, also known as neurotoxins (like Botox, Dysport, Xeomin, and Daxxify), bind to nerve receptors in unique ways, which means your body may become resistant to one but respond well to another. Because more people are starting neurotoxin treatments earlier in life and maintaining them consistently, resistance is becoming increasingly common.#faq-a9

mainEntity.name: What non-surgical treatments help tighten skin?#faq-q10

acceptedAnswer.text: Skin tightening comes from treatments that stimulate collagen through controlled injury. Mechanical treatments like microneedling, dermapen, and micro-coring create tiny micro-injuries in the skin, encouraging collagen production and improving texture. Thermal treatments such as Halo or CO2 lasers use heat to resurface the skin and tighten early laxity while refining pigmentation and fine lines. Chemical peels remain one of the most cost-effective and reliable ways to exfoliate damaged skin, address discoloration, and stimulate collagen for smoother, brighter results.#faq-a10

mainEntity.name: Should I get a lip flip, lip filler, or lip lift?#faq-q11

acceptedAnswer.text: Filler adds volume, a lip flip increases lip show, and a lip lift shortens an elongated upper lip. Lip filler is ideal for shaping, defining the borders, and adding soft volume, especially for patients wanting a fuller appearance without surgery. A lip flip uses small amounts of a wrinkle relaxer such as Botox to gently relax the upper lip muscle, allowing the lip to roll slightly outward and show more without adding volume. A lip flip is great for treating “gummy smiles”. A lip lift is a surgical procedure designed for patients with an elongated distance between the nose and upper lip, providing a permanent lift that filler or Botox cannot mimic. Younger patients are generally not good candidates for lip lifts because they tend to scar more visibly and rarely have the anatomical changes that make the surgery beneficial.#faq-a11

mainEntity.name: Which lasts longer—filler or fat grafting?#faq-q12

acceptedAnswer.text: Fat grafting offers long-lasting results, while filler is temporary but better near the mouth. Filler is ideal for the lips and areas with frequent movement, where fat may not survive as well or may feel firmer than desired. Fat grafting is best for the cheeks, temples, under eyes, and hands because these areas benefit from the soft, natural fullness that fat provides. Unlike filler, transferred fat can improve the quality of the overlying skin due to its regenerative properties, making it both restorative and long-lasting.#faq-a12

This article explains how factors like body frame, chest width, natural breast tissue, implant volume, and implant profile all influence breast augmentation results. It also shares practical tips for selecting implant size, including using goal photos, exploring real patient results with the Bustmob GoalFinder, and working closely with a plastic surgeon to create realistic expectations. keywords: how to choose breast implant size
best breast implant size for my body
what size breast implants should I get

@graph.articleBody: Choosing Your Breast Implant Size Choosing to undergo breast augmentation surgery is an exciting and personal decision. But once you've decided you're ready to move forward, a big question still remains: How do you choose your implant size? This part of the process can feel overwhelming for many. With so many sizes and shapes available—not to mention different profiles, volumes, and body types to consider—it’s easy to get lost in the options. But the good news is, you don’t have to figure it out alone. With the right tools, resources, and guidance, you can choose the right breast implant size for you with confidence. Whether you’re looking for a subtle enhancement or dreaming of larger breasts, this guide will walk you through what you need to know about breast implant sizing, what matters most, how to visualize your results, and how to work with your plastic surgeon to get the look you love. First, Let’s Talk About Size: It's More Than Just Cup Size One of the biggest misconceptions around breast augmentation is the idea that implants are chosen based on bra cup size. In reality, plastic surgeons use implant volume, measured in cubic centimeters (ccs), to determine size. Here’s why that matters: A 300cc implant can look very different on someone who is 5'1 and 110 pounds compared to someone who is 5'9 and 160 pounds. Your body frame, chest wall, natural breast tissue, and overall body type will all influence how implants look on you. That’s why the same implant size can produce dramatically different results for different people. So when you ask, What is the smallest breast implant size?, the answer may depend on your body and goals. But technically, implants start around 100cc and can go over 800cc. Tip #1: Look for Results on Bodies Like Yours Here’s the #1 tip for how to decide on breast implant size: find goal photos on people with similar body types to yours. Not models or celebrities—real people who’ve had breast augmentation and share your general frame, height, and weight. These photos can give you a much clearer and more realistic expectation of how various implant sizes and profiles such as high profile implants will actually look on you. This process helps you understand how factors like bra sizing, chest width, body shape, and natural breast anatomy can all influence your final result. We call these images goal photos—and they’re essential. Tip #2: Bring 3–5 Goal Photos to Your Consultation Once you find 3–5 goal photos that show the kind of look you love, bring them to your consultation. Your surgeon isn’t a mind-reader, and showing them visual examples will take the guesswork out of the process. It also helps them understand what natural means to you. One person's natural might be another’s too big or too small. When your plastic surgeon can see the results you’re aiming for, they’ll better understand your aesthetic goals and can match your desired look with the appropriate implant volume, shape, and profile. Tip #3: Use the Bustmob GoalFinder Finding realistic goal photos used to be time-consuming. That’s where the Bustmob GoalFinder comes in. This powerful tool is a unique database of thousands of real patient results, searchable by height, weight, and even implant size. So you can filter by your own stats and only see results from people who look like you who have undergone breast augmentation surgery. You can also save your favorite results into a personalized collection with a unique that is easily shared your surgeon and Patient Care Coordinator. This is a huge time-saver and makes it much easier to clearly communicate your goals during your consult. Pro Tip: Don’t just look for your dream size—look for examples of what you don’t want, too. Knowing what you don’t like is just as valuable in narrowing down the perfect implant size for your body. It’s important to remember every person’s body frame, chest wall, skin elasticity, and healing journeys are different. Even if you get the same size and brand of implant, your results are likely to be a little different than the person in the photo. Goal photos help show your plastic surgeon the look and general size you’re hoping for. From there, you’ll work together to set realistic expectation and create a plan that honors your anatomy and your goals. Understanding Implant Volume, Shape, and Profile We mentioned earlier that implants are sized in cubic centimeters (ccs). A rough estimate is that every 150–200ccs equals about one to one-and-a-half cup sizes, though cup sizes vary between brands and even between styles in the same brand. That’s why bra cup size is a loose reference point—not a precise science. When choosing implant volume, your plastic surgeon will also consider: Body frame: Smaller frames may not accommodate wider or larger implants comfortably or naturally. Chest wall: A wide or narrow chest may influence which implant shape or profile will look best. Breast tissue: Your current natural breast volume affects how an implant will sit and how natural the result will look. Your Aesthetic Goals: Some want a subtle, natural breast result, while others want larger sizes for a more dramatic change. You’ll also talk about implant profile. Implant profile refers to how far the implant projects outward from your chest. For example: Low profile: Wider base, less projection Moderate profile: Balanced base and projection High profile implants: Narrow base, more projection Multiple factors like anatomy, your goals, and desired size play into choosing the right implant profile. Your surgeon is there to guide you after an exam and thorough conversation about your goals. Considering the Long Term Here’s something that often brings peace of mind: You don’t have to get it perfect the first time. Choosing your implant size is important, but it’s not a one-time, permanent decision you’re stuck with forever. If down the road you decide you want a different look, whether that means going bigger, smaller, switching from saline to silicone or vice versa, changing brands, or even adjusting the implant profile, implant exchange is often a straightforward solution with typically a shorter recovery and results timeline. In fact, many people choose to update their implants as their lifestyle, aesthetic preferences, or body changes. So while it’s great to feel confident in your choice now, you don’t need to put pressure on yourself to predict exactly how you’ll feel 10 or 15 years from today. The most important thing is choosing something that feels right for right now. Your surgeon can walk you through what’s possible in the future, so you feel empowered—not locked in—as you move forward with your breast augmentation journey. Bra Cups Can Be Misleading It’s worth repeating: bra cup sizes are not a reliable way to choose implant size. One brand’s C cup is another brand’s D. And implants may fit differently in sports bras versus traditional underwire bras. That’s why plastic surgeons don’t often measure implants by cup size, but by volume in ccs. When you're thinking in terms of cup sizes like going from an A to a full C, it's best to express this as a goal photo rather than a lettered size. Your body shape, breast width, and current tissue play a huge role in how the implants will actually look in bras. How to Decide on Breast Implant Size—Step-by-Step Here’s a quick summary of the process: Step 1: Know your body Get familiar with your body frame, height, weight, chest wall, and natural breast shape. These will all influence the best implant size for you. Step 2: Use the Bustmob GoalFinder Find women with similar stats and browse their breast augmentation surgery results. Save 3–5 goal photos of looks you love, and a few photos of looks you want to avoid. Step 3: Book your consultation Bring your photos and talk through your goals, lifestyle, and concerns. You may also have the opportunity to use 3D imaging to virtually try on different sizes at your consultation. Choosing your breast implant size is one of the most exciting, and personal, parts of the journey. Don’t rush it. Take your time, do your research, and remember that there is no perfect size—just the size that’s perfect for you. With the help of tools like GoalFinder, a trusted plastic surgeon, and a thoughtful look at your lifestyle and goals, you’ll be well-equipped to choose a size that helps you feel confident, balanced, and beautiful for years to come.

itemListElement.name: Why Does Loose Skin Occur After Weight Loss?

@graph.abstract: Significant weight loss can leave behind loose, excess skin due to stretched collagen and elastin fibers that no longer fully retract after fat loss. This article explains why skin laxity occurs after weight loss, the factors that influence its severity—including age, genetics, rate of weight loss, and overall skin health—and the emotional and physical impact excess skin can have on confidence, comfort, and daily life. It also explores why diet and exercise alone cannot remove redundant skin and outlines the most effective plastic surgery options for post-weight loss body contouring, including tummy tuck, body lift, arm lift, thigh lift, breast procedures, and upper body lift surgery.
keywords: skin removal surgery after weight loss

@graph.articleBody: Why Loose Skin Occurs After Weight Loss Our skin is a remarkable organ with a degree of elasticity, allowing it to stretch and contract in response to changes in body size. However, when we experience prolonged periods of weight gain, the skin's elastic fibers—primarily collagen and elastin—can become overstretched and damaged. These vital proteins are responsible for your skin's firmness and ability to snap back. Think of it like a balloon that has been inflated to its maximum capacity for an extended period; even after the air is released, it may not fully return to its original tautness. Similarly, the prolonged expansion of the skin can lead to structural changes that compromise its natural recoil. When significant weight loss occurs, whether through gradual lifestyle changes, bariatric surgery, or GLP1 medications like Wegovy, Tirzepatide, or Ozempic, the underlying fat volume decreases. While the skin may initially contract to some extent, the damage to the elastic fibers and the sheer amount of previously stretched skin can prevent it from fully retracting. This results in the characteristic appearance of loose, sagging skin, often referred to as excess skin, skin laxity, or redundant skin. What Factors Influence the Degree of Loose Skin After Significant Weight Loss? The extent to which loose skin develops after shedding a significant amount of weight is highly individualized and influenced by a combination of factors. Understanding these can help set realistic expectations and inform treatment considerations. How Does Aging Affect Skin Elasticity and Recovery After Weight Loss? As we age, our skin naturally loses some of its inherent elasticity. This is due to a natural slowdown in the production of collagen and elastin, the very proteins that give skin its youthful firmness and resilience. Older individuals, therefore, may find it more challenging for their skin to bounce back after substantial weight loss compared to younger individuals with more robust collagen and elastin reserves. The cumulative effects of sun exposure and environmental factors over time also contribute to this decline in skin quality. Does My Genetic Makeup Determine How Much Loose Skin I'll Have? Our individual genetic makeup plays a significant role in our skin's inherent quality and elasticity. Some people are naturally predisposed to having more resilient, thicker skin with a higher capacity for retraction, while others may have skin that is naturally less elastic. This genetic predisposition can significantly influence the degree of loose skin experienced, even with similar weight loss journeys. Amount and Duration of Weight Gain: How Do My Starting Weight and How Long I Was Overweight Impact Loose Skin? The more weight you carried and the longer you carried it, the more likely you are to have significant excess skin after weight loss. Prolonged distension of the skin can lead to more extensive damage to the collagen and elastin fibers, making it harder for the skin to retract once the underlying fat is gone. For individuals who have maintained a significant amount of excess weight for many years, the likelihood of substantial loose skin is generally higher. Rate of Weight Loss: Is Losing Weight Quickly More Likely to Cause Loose Skin? While rapid weight loss can be incredibly motivating, it may not allow the skin sufficient time to gradually adapt and contract to the changing body contours. Slower, more sustainable weight loss (e.g., 1–2 pounds per week) can sometimes result in better skin retraction, as it provides a more gradual transition. However, it's important to note that even with a slow and steady approach, significant weight loss (e.g., 50 pounds or more) often still leads to some degree of skin laxity that may require surgical intervention. What Role Do Hydration, Nutrition, and Habits Play in Skin Firmness? Your overall health profoundly impacts skin health and its ability to recover. Hydration: Well-hydrated skin is more supple and resilient. Nutrition: A diet rich in protein, vitamins (especially C and E), and minerals (like zinc and copper) provides the essential building blocks for collagen and elastin production. Smoking: Smoking significantly degrades collagen and elastin, accelerating skin aging and reducing its elasticity. Sun Exposure: Excessive unprotected sun exposure damages skin fibers, contributing to laxity. Medical Conditions: For individuals managing chronic conditions like type 2 diabetes, optimizing overall health and blood sugar control is crucial for skin integrity and healing processes. Addressing the Emotional and Physical Impact of Excess Skin After Weight Loss It's crucial to acknowledge that the presence of loose skin after achieving your weight loss goals is a normal physiological outcome for many individuals on their weight loss journey. While the profound health benefits of weight loss are paramount, it's also entirely valid to feel frustrated, self-conscious, or even disheartened about the excess skin. This excess skin can lead to a variety of challenges, including physical discomfort such as chafing and skin irritation, difficulty with hygiene and increased risk of rashes or infections, limited mobility during exercise or daily activities, and back pain or postural issues due to the weight of hanging skin. It can also create clothing challenges, making it difficult to find clothes that fit well and feel comfortable, and contribute to feeling that your outer appearance does not fully reflect your internal transformation. Emotionally, it may impact body image, self-esteem, and confidence, and lead to feelings of incompleteness or social anxiety. Recognizing these feelings is the first step toward exploring solutions that help you feel more comfortable and confident. At Amelia Aesthetics, personalized consultations are available to discuss surgical options for post-weight loss body contouring. Can You Address Excess Skin with Diet and Exercise After Weight Loss? While a balanced diet and active lifestyle are essential for maintaining weight loss and supporting skin health, they have limitations when it comes to existing loose skin. Once skin has been significantly stretched and elasticity is lost, diet and exercise alone cannot effectively remove excess skin. These methods can improve muscle tone and overall health but do not address redundant skin. Plastic surgery is often the most effective solution for individuals with significant loose skin after major weight loss. Body contouring procedures are designed to remove excess skin and reshape the body for a more toned and proportionate appearance. How Can Plastic Surgery Help Remove Excess Skin and Fat After Weight Loss? Plastic surgery offers a range of procedures tailored to address the body after significant weight loss. These procedures remove excess skin and reshape underlying tissues to create a smoother, firmer appearance. Common procedures include abdominoplasty (tummy tuck) to address excess abdominal skin and tighten muscles, arm lift (brachioplasty) to contour the upper arms, thigh lift to improve leg contours, body lift to address the lower body, breast reduction to relieve discomfort and improve proportion, breast lift to restore position and shape, and upper body lift to address excess skin on the back. These procedures are often performed in stages as part of a personalized plan. At Amelia Aesthetics, experienced plastic surgeons help patients achieve results that reflect their weight loss journey, with consultations focused on individualized recommendations and goals.

@graph.articleBody: If you’ve ever wondered, Am I too young for a facelift?—you’re not alone. Many patients in their 40s or early 50s start noticing subtle changes like sagging skin, early jowls, or deeper smile lines and begin exploring their options. These changes can be a result of aging, genetics, weight loss, or a combination of all three. Because everyone’s goals and anatomy are different, there’s no single perfect age for a facelift procedure, but understanding how aging, genetics, and weight loss affect your facial structures and knowing the benefits of a deep plane facelift can help you make an informed, confident decision. Let’s dive into the timing, benefits, and options available when it comes to facial rejuvenation with plastic surgery. A deep plane facelift is one of the most advanced facelift techniques available today. Unlike a traditional facelift, which focuses mainly on tightening the skin, the deep plane approach lifts and repositions deeper layers of tissue, including the muscle and connective structures beneath the surface. This technique addresses common signs of facial aging such as hollowing of the temples, prominent orbital ridge, mid-face volume loss, loss of cheekbone contour, deep nasolabial folds, downturn of the mouth, jawline sagging, jowling, and chin flattening. Because deeper facial structures are repositioned, results tend to look more natural and avoid an overly tight appearance, while also offering longer-lasting outcomes. At Amelia, a facelift often includes a neck lift and fat grafting as needed to create balanced, natural-looking results. The best age for a facelift depends on your anatomy, lifestyle, and aesthetic goals. Some patients consider surgery in their 40s to address early aging or after weight loss, while others wait until their 50s or 60s when sagging and skin laxity become more pronounced. There is no one-size-fits-all approach, but factors such as genetics, sun exposure, skin quality, facial volume loss, and personal preferences all influence timing. In your 40s, early signs of aging may include mild skin laxity, developing jowls, deeper smile lines, and mid-face volume loss. A facelift at this stage can provide subtle, natural-looking rejuvenation and help maintain a youthful appearance over time, often with the benefit of better skin elasticity and recovery. In your 50s and 60s, facelift surgery is more common as facial aging becomes more visible, with increased skin laxity, deeper folds, and more significant volume loss. A deep plane facelift combined with a neck lift and fat grafting can restore definition and harmony, creating comprehensive and natural results. While results may be more dramatic at this stage, reduced skin elasticity can impact healing and outcomes. Ultimately, the right age for a deep plane facelift is a personal decision based on your unique anatomy and goals. A consultation provides the opportunity to receive personalized recommendations, understand your options, and make a confident, informed choice in a supportive, pressure-free environment.

@graph.articleBody: Congratulations on taking a significant step towards a healthier and happier you! The journey to lose weight is a testament to your dedication and commitment to well-being. You’ve navigated the challenges, celebrated the milestones, and are now experiencing the incredible benefits of your hard work. This is a moment to truly acknowledge and applaud your remarkable achievement. As you meet your weight loss goals, you might notice another common outcome of rapid weight loss: loose or excess skin. This is a natural physiological response and is very common for those who have achieved substantial weight loss, regardless of the method. While the health benefits of weight and fat loss are undeniable, the presence of excess skin can sometimes hinder the full realization of your goals, both physically and emotionally. Know that you are not alone in this. Many individuals who have successfully lost weight through various means, including GLP-1 therapies like Ozempic, Wegovy, and Mounjaro, may consider options to address excess skin. Common Plastic Surgery Procedures After Weight Loss Plastic surgery offers a range of surgical procedures specifically designed to remove excess skin and refine body contours following significant weight loss. These procedures aim to remove excess skin and underlying fat, tighten the remaining tissues, and create a smoother, more toned appearance that aligns with your weight loss success. Here are some common procedures after weight loss: Abdomen: Tummy Tuck (Abdominoplasty): This is one of the most frequently considered procedures after significant weight loss, particularly for addressing excess skin and fat in the abdominal area. A tummy tuck involves surgically removing the redundant skin and fat between the pubic bone and the ribcage. At Amelia, a tummy tuck often includes waistline liposuction, muscle repair, and Exparel, at no additional cost. Body Lifts (Circumferential Body Lift/Belt Lipectomy): For individuals with significant loose skin extending around the entire torso, including the abdomen, flanks, lower back, and buttocks, body lifts may be recommended. This more extensive procedure essentially removes a belt of excess skin and fat, lifting and tightening the entire midsection. Breasts: Breast Lifts (Mastopexy): Weight loss can often lead to a loss of volume and sagging in the breasts as the underlying fat decreases and the skin loses elasticity. Breast lifts address this by removing excess skin and reshaping the remaining breast tissue to create a more youthful and lifted appearance. The nipples and areolae are often repositioned to a higher position. In some cases, a breast lift can be combined with breast implants (breast augmentation) to restore lost volume and enhance shape. At Amelia, a breast lift often includes bra line liposuction at no additional cost to enhance breast shape and the overall result. Breast Reduction (Reduction Mammaplasty): For some individuals, particularly after significant weight loss, the breasts may remain large and feel heavy, contributing to discomfort and a disproportionate figure. Breast reduction surgery addresses this by surgically removing excess breast tissue, fat, and skin. Similar to a breast lift, a breast reduction at Amelia often includes bra line liposuction. The goal of this procedure is to alleviate physical symptoms like back, neck, and shoulder pain, improve bra fit, and enhance overall comfort, especially during exercise. Beyond size reduction, the remaining breast tissue is reshaped to create a more balanced and lifted contour. As part of the procedure, the nipples and areolae are often repositioned to a more natural location on the smaller, reshaped breasts. Arms: Arm Lifts (Brachioplasty): Excess, sagging skin on the upper arms, often referred to as bat wings, is a common concern after significant weight loss. Arm lifts surgically remove excess skin and underlying fat, resulting in a more toned and defined arm contour. Thighs: Thigh Lifts (Thighplasty): Loose skin on the inner or outer thighs can be a source of discomfort and make clothing fit poorly. Thigh lifts address this by removing excess skin and fat from the thighs, improving their shape and reducing sagging. Face and Neck: Facelift and Neck Lift: While weight loss can be beneficial for overall facial appearance, significant weight fluctuations can sometimes contribute to “Ozempic Face,” sagging skin in the face and neck. A facelift is a surgical procedure that tightens the facial skin and underlying tissues, reducing wrinkles and improving the overall contour of the face and jawline. At Amelia, a facelift also includes a neck lift. A neck lift can address excess skin and fat in the neck area, often referred to as a turkey neck, improving jawline definition and reducing neck bands. It's important to note that these procedures can often be combined to address multiple areas of concern simultaneously, depending on your individual needs and the surgeon's recommendations. Remember that diet and exercise play a crucial role in maintaining your weight loss results after any surgical procedure. Starting Your Surgical Journey The journey towards addressing excess skin begins with a comprehensive consultation with a plastic surgeon who specializes in post-weight loss body contouring. Choosing a surgeon with specific experience in this area is crucial, as they will have a deep understanding of the unique challenges and considerations involved. During your consultation, you can expect the surgeon to: Review your medical history and weight loss journey: This includes discussing the amount of weight you lost, the methods you used (including GLP-1 medications or bariatric surgery), and any existing medical conditions, such as type 2 diabetes. Perform a physical examination: The surgeon will assess the amount and location of your excess skin and evaluate your skin elasticity and overall tissue quality. Discuss your goals, expectations, and concerns: This is your opportunity to articulate what you hope to achieve with plastic surgery and to ask any questions you may have regarding the surgery procedures. Explain suitable surgical options and potential outcomes: Based on your individual anatomy and goals, the surgeon will outline the procedures that may be appropriate for you, discuss the expected results, and help set realistic expectations. Review the risks, benefits, and recovery process: It's essential to have a clear understanding of the potential risks and complications associated with each procedure, as well as the expected recovery timeline and post-operative care instructions. Answer all your questions thoroughly: Don't hesitate to ask anything you're unsure about. A good surgeon will ensure you feel informed and comfortable with the proposed plan to remove excess skin. The outcome of this consultation will be a personalized treatment plan tailored to your specific needs and desires to address excess skin. Your surgeon may recommend a single procedure or a combination of procedures, potentially staged over time, to achieve the best possible results after your significant weight loss. Your journey to lose weight with the aid of GLP-1 medications is a remarkable testament to your commitment to a healthier life. You've achieved something truly significant, and you deserve to feel confident and comfortable in your own skin. If excess skin is preventing you from fully embracing your new body and enjoying the fruits of your labor after your significant weight loss, know that you have options. Plastic surgery after weight loss offers a powerful way to address excess skin and further enhance your well-being. By exploring the surgery procedures available and consulting with a plastic surgeon, you can take the next step in your transformative journey and embrace your new body with pride and confidence.

@graph.articleBody: My weight gain started right after I graduated high school. People who have never experienced major weight gain love to oversimplify how one actually becomes obese. It isn’t just sitting on the couch and overeating, though that certainly played a part in my journey. As I’m sure anyone who’s been obese will tell you, my path to being almost 300 pounds was more than just overeating. It was a blend of things in and out of my control. To start, I had a terrible relationship with food. I grew up very poor with my single mom and younger siblings. There were a lot of times when we didn’t have a lot of food to eat. Maybe that’s what fueled my desire to keep eating as an adult—knowing there was food now and I should take advantage of it. As the oldest kid, I was expected to play a role in raising my siblings. I spent my childhood caring for them without a ton of support from the adults in my life. The stress of parenting my younger siblings paved the way for anxiety and depression. The medication I was prescribed to manage my depression came with side effects, two major ones being hormonal changes that further fueled my PCOS and weight gain. PCOS, polycystic ovary syndrome, is a lifelong and hereditary disease—almost every woman in my family has it and it also comes with a long list of side effects including weight gain and increased risk of type 2 diabetes and other chronic health conditions. I started noticing the effects of PCOS in high school. It was slow at first, but as soon as I graduated, the weight just came and stayed. Within three years of graduating high school, I put on 100 pounds. It didn’t help that after graduation I lived with people who enjoyed cooking sweets, desserts, and carbs. Though if I’m being honest, it wasn’t even so much what I was eating, it was how much I was eating. When everything felt dark and gloomy and I felt very alone, food was comfortable. Everything combined just kind of blew up, and before I knew it I was almost 300 pounds. My weight loss journey started at rock bottom. I was pregnant with our youngest when I found out my husband was cheating on me. As dark as it sounds, if he didn’t do what he did, I wouldn’t be here today in this self-love era of wanting to improve. Neither of us came from a healthy family environment and it showed in how we managed our relationship. We had no money, were living with family, and had jobs that barely paid the bills. I was overweight and struggling to play with our toddler without getting tired. I was battling anxiety and depression and felt like I was at rock bottom at 24 years old. The infidelity sparked a fire in me. I was tired of choosing everyone else and not myself. I realized I needed help. We both started individual therapy and couples therapy. My therapist told me I didn’t love myself, and she was right. I had never learned how. With therapy, I began to trust myself, validate myself, and believe I was worthy. Slowly, I started to heal. My husband started to heal. We started to heal together. From there, things began to fall into place. I kept seeing people on TikTok going through weight loss surgery and wanted more for myself. I decided it was time to make my weight loss dream a reality. It’s been three years since I had gastric bypass surgery. Anyone who hasn’t gone through it thinks it’s easy, but it’s one of the most physically and mentally demanding things you can do. I lost 60 pounds before plateauing and then worked with a personal trainer to lose another 70 pounds, totaling 145 pounds lost. After losing over 100 pounds, I noticed people treated me differently, which was frustrating. I had to lose that much weight just for people to see me as powerful. After losing over 140 pounds, I felt skinny but still didn’t feel good due to loose skin and body dysmorphia. I was 28 with excess skin that didn’t reflect how hard I had worked. I felt driven to become my best self and was inspired by Dr. Hunter’s work. I booked a consultation and began my skin removal journey. I’ve had two phases of surgery in Raleigh, NC. My first was a mommy makeover including a breast lift with implants and a tummy tuck with liposuction and muscle repair. The experience and aftercare exceeded my expectations. My second surgery was a thigh lift to remove excess skin and fat. Recovery was manageable, even as a working mom with two kids. For me, skin removal was necessary. Losing weight gave me health, but removing the excess skin gave me confidence. I feel like I can finally show the world who I am. If someone says I’m not the same person, they’re right. That’s what transformation is. I used to hide, avoid attention, and lack confidence. Now I want to be seen, heard, and live fully. The past three years have been fast and full of growth—weight loss, surgery, healing my marriage, buying a house, and advancing my career. While the infidelity was painful, it sparked the change that led me here. I wouldn’t choose a different path. Through this journey, I’ve realized my worth and potential. The weight loss was for me, but the lifestyle is for my daughters. I want them to see what it looks like to love and care for yourself and to know they can achieve anything. Everything I do now is for them and future generations. The growth is beautiful and worth it, and I want every woman to know they deserve to feel confident and empowered in their own body.
